CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) is a powerful tool for precise editing of DNA sequences in living organisms. It works by guiding a Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA location using a complementary RNA sequence, where it creates a double-strand break that can be repaired to insert, delete, or replace genetic material [Source 2: biotechnology-genomics.txt]. This technology has been used for various clinical applications, including the treatment of sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia [Source 1: biotechnology-genomics.txt].

Additionally, CRISPR gene editing is also being explored for cancer immunotherapy and correction of genetic mutations causing inherited diseases. There are even more precise variants of CRISPR, such as base editing and prime editing, which can make single-letter changes to DNA without creating double-strand breaks [Source 1: biotechnology-genomics.txt].
